event: Taino and Lucayan peoples, Arawak-speaking Amerindians from Hispaniola, begin settling the islands, establishing fishing and farming communities.
Lucayan settlements flourish across the islands, with archaeological sites revealing villages, pottery, and salt-gathering practices.
Some traditions hold that Christopher Columbus's first landfall in the New World was at Grand Turk, though the exact site remains debated.
Spanish explorer Juan Ponce de Leon sights the islands during his expedition through the region.
Spanish slave raids and introduced diseases devastate the Lucayan population, leaving the islands largely depopulated within a few decades.
Bermudian salt collectors begin seasonal voyages to rake salt from the islands' natural salinas, founding the first European-linked settlements.
Spanish and French forces briefly occupy the salt-producing islands during the War of the Spanish Succession before Bermudians regain control.
France raids the islands' salt operations, prompting Britain to assert firmer authority over the territory.
The islands are formally placed under the jurisdiction of the Bahamas colonial administration.
American Loyalists fleeing the United States after the Revolutionary War settle the Caicos Islands, establishing cotton plantations worked by enslaved Africans.
The Turks and Caicos Islands are formally annexed to the Bahamas, though salt-raking Bermudians resist Bahamian control.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, affecting the plantation economy that relied on enslaved labour.
Declining soil fertility and hurricanes cause the cotton plantation economy to collapse, and many planters abandon the islands.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, emancipating the enslaved population of the islands.
The Turks and Caicos Islands are separated from the Bahamas and granted their own presidency under a Council and a President.
The islands are annexed to Jamaica as a dependency, ending their brief period of separate administration.
The post of Commissioner is established to administer the islands under Jamaican oversight.
Canadian Prime Minister Robert Borden suggests Canada annex the islands, an idea revived periodically but never adopted.
Salt production, long the economic mainstay, continues to decline as the industry faces shrinking markets.
The islands gain a separate administration again while remaining linked to Jamaica through a shared Governor.
When Jamaica becomes independent, the Turks and Caicos Islands become a British Crown colony administered from the Bahamas.
The Governor of the Bahamas oversees the islands, with a Commissioner managing local affairs.
Following Bahamian independence, the Turks and Caicos receive their own Governor and become a distinct British dependent territory.
The islands adopt their first constitution and gain self-government, with James Alexander George Smith McCartney becoming the first Chief Minister.
Chief Minister J.A.G.S. McCartney dies in a plane crash, a major loss for the territory's emerging political leadership.
Construction begins on Club Med on Providenciales, marking the start of the islands' transformation into a tourism destination.
Providenciales International Airport opens its runway, accelerating tourism development on the island.
Chief Minister Norman Saunders is arrested in Miami on drug-related charges, triggering a major political scandal.
Britain suspends the constitution and imposes direct rule after a commission finds evidence of corruption and arson in government.
Ministerial government is restored following the period of direct rule, with new elections held.
Tourism surpasses fishing and salt as the dominant sector of the economy, centred on Providenciales' Grace Bay beaches.
Offshore financial services expand significantly, complementing tourism as a pillar of the economy.
The British Overseas Territories Act grants full British citizenship to islanders, reflecting the territory's revised status.
A new constitution comes into force, increasing local autonomy and renaming the Chief Minister as Premier.
Hurricanes Hanna and Ike strike the islands, causing extensive damage, especially on Grand Turk and South Caicos.
Britain suspends the constitution and imposes direct rule after a commission of inquiry finds systemic corruption under Premier Michael Misick.
Self-government is restored following constitutional reforms, and general elections return an elected legislature and Premier.
Tourism reaches record levels, with Providenciales established as a leading luxury Caribbean destination.
Hurricanes Irma and Maria batter the islands within weeks, causing widespread destruction and prompting major recovery efforts.
The COVID-19 pandemic halts international tourism, severely impacting the islands' economy before a phased reopening.
Washington Misick of the Progressive National Party becomes Premier after the party wins general elections.
The territory grapples with rising migration and security challenges while continuing post-pandemic tourism recovery.
